oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with feature-specific fields) -> stdout exactly matching public_test_cases expected_output

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The keyboard shortcut to enable the default 'send_event' mode is Ctrl+K. Otherwise, the keyword arguments are forwarded directly to the transition's callback signature.
When 'is_state=active_user' is queried, the output displays 'value=true' since the active state is 'active_user', otherwise 'value=false'.

This generated package must cover the full detailed-PRD behavior represented by all authoritative public test case files, not just a subset mentioned in the fuzzy PRD. Public feature files: feature1_basic_transitions, feature2_conditional_transitions, feature3_lifecycle_callbacks, feature4_event_payload, feature5_auto_transitions, feature6_ordered_transitions. Feature descriptions include: A machine is created over a set of named states with one designated as the starting state. Explicitly declared transitions wire a trigger name to a (source -> destination) edge. Firing a trigger while in the matching source state moves the machine to the destination state; the active state can be queried directly, and per-state predicates report whether a given state is the current one. Each fired trigger reports whether it caused a move and the resulting active state.; A transition can be guarded by conditions. A `conditions` guard requires every named boolean check to return true for the transition to fire; an `unless` guard requires every named check to return false. When a guard blocks a transition, firing the trigger reports no move and the active state is unchanged. When several transitions share the same trigger and source, they are evaluated in declaration order and the first whose guards all pass is taken. `cond_pass` is a check that always returns true and `cond_fail` always returns false.; States can carry lifecycle callbacks that fire as a side effect of transitions: an enter callback runs when a state is entered, an exit callback runs when a state is left. Callbacks are referenced by name and mutate observable model data. `greet` records the text 'Hello World!' and `farewell` records the text 'So long, suckers!'. Enter/exit callbacks can be registered after the machine is built, or declared inline on a state at construction time. The recorded message can be read back to confirm a callback fired.; Triggers may carry a payload of positional/keyword arguments. The machine has a payload-delivery mode controlling how that payload reaches transition callbacks. When delivery is disabled, the trigger's keyword arguments are forwarded directly to the callback signature; `store_kwarg_message` records the value supplied under the keyword `message`. When delivery is enabled, callbacks instead receive a single event object that wraps the arguments; `store_event_message` reads the same `message` value out of that event object's keyword payload. Either way the recorded message reflects the value passed at trigger time.; When automatic transitions are enabled, the machine provides a convenience trigger named `to_<state>` for every registered state that moves to that state from any source. When automatic transitions are disabled, no such convenience trigger exists and attempting to fire one is reported as a missing-trigger error. The active state is reported after each successful automatic transition.; A helper wires up a chain of transitions that step linearly through a sequence of states under a single advancing trigger (default name `next_state`). By default it loops the last state back to the first. When no starting state is supplied at construction, an implicit placeholder starting state is created and included in the chain. The loop-back target can be configured to skip that placeholder. The chain can also be built from an explicit custom ordering with a custom trigger name, or requested directly at construction time. Each advance reports the resulting active state.

The execution adapter must accept exactly the input shapes used by public_test_cases and emit stdout exactly equal to each expected_output. Observed top-level input fields/values include: machine=['{dict}'], commands=['[list]']. Do not require a different discriminator field, different action names, JSON output where line output is expected, or extra PASS/FAIL metadata.

The embedded public_test_cases are part of oracle_data and are authoritative for exact input shapes, action/operation names, stdout line order, whitespace, error categories, numeric formatting, and deterministic serialization. If prose, fuzzy_prd, context pointers, or older hidden constraints are vague or conflict with expected_output, the public_test_cases expected_output is the pass-critical contract.

Ignore any vague analogy or context pointer that names an unrelated module, pipeline, login flow, audit generator, or previous project. Such wording is non-normative; only this repo’s detailed PRD, FULL_SCOPE, EXECUTION_CONTRACT, and public_test_cases define the task.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: References the deep object structure format for the overall state tree stored within the registry, specifically how state objects are nested versus flat arrays.
Refers to the specific boolean flag 'wrap_around' used in the Ordered Transitions loop configuration to determine if the chain returns to the initial state after the last one.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more detail on that — check the codebase or the existing test cases for the exact behavior.

feature1_basic_transitions.json.description: A machine is created over a set of named states with one designated as the starting state. Explicitly declared transitions wire a trigger name to a (source -> destination) edge. Firing a trigger while in the matching source state moves the machine to the destination state; the active state can be queried directly, and per-state predicates report whether a given state is the current one. Each fired trigger reports whether it caused a move and the resulting active state.

cases.expected_output:
trigger=advance result=true state=B
state=B
is_state=A value=false
is_state=B value=true
trigger=advance result=true state=C
state=C

feature2_conditional_transitions.json.description: A transition can be guarded by conditions. A `conditions` guard requires every named boolean check to return true for the transition to fire; an `unless` guard requires every named check to return false. When a guard blocks a transition, firing the trigger reports no move and the active state is unchanged. When several transitions share the same trigger and source, they are evaluated in declaration order and the first whose guards all pass is taken. `cond_pass` is a check that always returns true and `cond_fail` always returns false.

cases.expected_output:
trigger=advance result=true state=B
state=B
trigger=advance result=true state=C
state=C
trigger=advance result=false state=C
state=C

feature3_lifecycle_callbacks.json.description: States can carry lifecycle callbacks that fire as a side effect of transitions: an enter callback runs when a state is entered, an exit callback runs when a state is left. Callbacks are referenced by name and mutate observable model data. `greet` records the text 'Hello World!' and `farewell` records the text 'So long, suckers!'. Enter/exit callbacks can be registered after the machine is built, or declared inline on a state at construction time. The recorded message can be read back to confirm a callback fired.

cases.expected_output:
trigger=advance result=true state=B
message=Hello World!
trigger=reverse result=true state=A
message=So long, suckers!

feature4_event_payload.json.description: Triggers may carry a payload of positional/keyword arguments. The machine has a payload-delivery mode controlling how that payload reaches transition callbacks. When delivery is disabled, the trigger's keyword arguments are forwarded directly to the callback signature; `store_kwarg_message` records the value supplied under the keyword `message`. When delivery is enabled, callbacks instead receive a single event object that wraps the arguments; `store_event_message` reads the same `message` value out of that event object's keyword payload. Either way the recorded message reflects the value passed at trigger time.

kwargs.message: Hallo. My name is Inigo Montoya.

cases.expected_output:
trigger=advance result=true state=B
message=Hallo. My name is Inigo Montoya.

kwargs.message: You killed my father. Prepare to die.

cases.expected_output:
trigger=advance result=true state=B
message=You killed my father. Prepare to die.

feature5_auto_transitions.json.description: When automatic transitions are enabled, the machine provides a convenience trigger named `to_<state>` for every registered state that moves to that state from any source. When automatic transitions are disabled, no such convenience trigger exists and attempting to fire one is reported as a missing-trigger error. The active state is reported after each successful automatic transition.

cases.expected_output:
trigger=to_B result=true state=B
state=B
trigger=to_C result=true state=C
state=C
trigger=to_A result=true state=A
state=A

feature6_ordered_transitions.json.description: A helper wires up a chain of transitions that step linearly through a sequence of states under a single advancing trigger (default name `next_state`). By default it loops the last state back to the first. When no starting state is supplied at construction, an implicit placeholder starting state is created and included in the chain. The loop-back target can be configured to skip that placeholder. The chain can also be built from an explicit custom ordering with a custom trigger name, or requested directly at construction time. Each advance reports the resulting active state.

cases.expected_output:
state=initial
trigger=next_state result=true state=beginning
state=beginning
trigger=next_state result=true state=middle
trigger=next_state result=true state=end
state=end
trigger=next_state result=true state=initial
state=initial

trigger=advance result=true state=end
state=end
trigger=advance result=true state=beginning
state=beginning

_constraints.output_rule: Creates the state machine registries. If states include objects, their properties become embedded.
Strings are treated as state names. Objects must have a 'name' string property. Objects may also have 'on_enter' (string) and 'on_exit' (string) properties.
Removes the trusty command selected transition triggers to failure triggers named 'to_<state>'. The active state is set to the initial state.
Maps trigger name to source state, destination state, optional conditions array, optional unless array, and optional callback hooks for guard evaluation.

_constraints.input_condition: Trigger fired where source matches; 'conditions' guard exists
_constraints.output_rule: Transition fires only if all named boolean checks return true.

_constraints.input_condition: Trigger fired where source matches; 'unless' guard exists
_constraints.output_rule: Transition fires only if all named boolean checks return false.

_constraints.input_condition: Multiple transitions share the same trigger and source state
_constraints.output_rule: Transitions are evaluated in declaration order. The first guard passing allows the transition and stops further evaluation for that trigger.

_constraints.input_condition: Transition occurs into state X or out of state Y
_constraints.output_rule: State X's 'on_enter' callback fires. State Y's 'on_exit' callback fires.
Disabled: Keyword arguments forwarded directly to the transition's callback signature.
Enabled: Callback receives a single event object. The keyword arguments act as properties on that event object.
Generates 'to_<StateName>' triggers for all states. These triggers fire regardless of source state and report success.

_constraints.input_condition: Machine flags 'auto_transitions' as false, or attempt to invoke auto trigger
_constraints.output_rule: If auto is disabled and trigger invoked, report missing trigger error. If trigger not declared manually and auto is disabled, report missing trigger error.
Output must contain: 'trigger=<name> result=true state=<destination>'.
Output must contain: 'trigger=<name> result=false state=<source>'.

_constraints.input_condition: Trigger name does not exist in any transition or auto-gen list
_constraints.output_rule: Output must contain: 'error=no_trigger trigger=<name>'.
Output the current active state on a new line.
Output the recorded message on a new line.
Output: 'is_state=<name> value=<true/false>'. Value is true iff active state matches <name>.
Uses default name: 'next_state'.
By default, the loop wraps back to the first state (or the initial state defined).

hide.oracle_response: The keyboard shortcut to enable the default 'send_event' mode is Ctrl+K. Otherwise, the keyword arguments are forwarded directly to the transition's callback signature.

hide.replacement: the current boolean state of the queried account
hide.oracle_response: When 'is_state=active_user' is queried, the output displays 'value=true' since the active state is 'active_user', otherwise 'value=false'.
